The proper Python path is looked up in a table of known\n    43→    # distros/versions with included Pythons; if nothing is found, depending on the discovery mode, either the\n    44→    # default fallback of /usr/bin/python is used (if we know it's there), or discovery fails.\n    45→\n    46→    # FUTURE: add logical equivalence for \"python3\" in the case of py3-only modules?\n    47→    if interpreter_name != 'python':\n    48→        raise ValueError('Interpreter discovery not supported for {0}'.format(interpreter_name))\n    49→\n    50→    host = task_vars.get('inventory_hostname', 'unknown')\n    51→    res = None\n    52→    platform_type = 'unknown'\n    53→    found_interpreters = [u'/usr/bin/python']  # not all command -v impls accept a list of commands, so we have to call it once per python\n    64→        command_list = [\"command -v '%s'\" % py for py in bootstrap_python_list]\n    65→        shell_bootstrap = \"echo PLATFORM; uname; echo FOUND; {0}; echo ENDFOUND\".format('; '.join(command_list))\n    66→\n    67→        # FUTURE: in most cases we probably don't want to use become, but maybe sometimes we do?\n    68→        res = action._low_level_execute_command(shell_bootstrap, sudoable=False)\n    69→\n    70→        raw_stdout = res.get('stdout', u'')\n    71→\n    72→        match = foundre.match(raw_stdout)\n    73→\n    74→        if not match:\n    75→            display.debug(u'raw interpreter discovery output: {0}'.format(raw_stdout), host=host)\n    76→            raise ValueError('unexpected output from Python interpreter discovery')\n    77→\n    78→        platform_type = match.groups()[0].lower().strip()\n    79→\n    80→        found_interpreters = [interp.strip() for interp in match.groups()[1].splitlines() if interp.startswith('/')]\n    81→\n    82→        display.debug(u\"found interpreters: {0}\".format(found_interpreters), host=host)\n    83→\n    84→        if not found_interpreters:\n    85→            action._discovery_warnings.append(u'No python interpreters found for host {0} (tried {1})'.format(host, bootstrap_python_list))\n    86→            # this is lame, but returning None or throwing an exception is uglier\n    87→            return u'/usr/bin/python'\n    88→\n    89→        if platform_type != 'linux':\n    90→            raise NotImplementedError('unsupported platform for extended discovery: {0}'.format(to_native(platform_type)))\n    91→\n    92→        platform_script = pkgutil.get_data('ansible.executor.discovery', 'python_target.py')\n    93→\n    94→        # FUTURE: respect pipelining setting instead of just if the connection supports it?\n    95→        if action._connection.has_pipelining:\n    96→            res = action._low_level_execute_command(found_interpreters[0], sudoable=False, in_data=platform_script)\n    97→        else:\n    98→            # First, we use a simple shell-agnostic bootstrap to\n    # get the system type from uname, and find any random Python that can get us the info we need. For supported\n    # target OS types, we'll dispatch a Python script that calls plaform.dist() (for older platforms, where available)\n    # and brings back /etc/os-release (if present). The proper Python path is looked up in a table of known\n    # distros/versions with included Pythons; if nothing is found, depending on the discovery mode, either the\n    # default fallback of /usr/bin/python is used (if we know it's there), or discovery fails.\n\n    # FUTURE: add logical equivalence for \"python3\" in the case of py3-only modules?\n    if interpreter_name != 'python':\n        raise ValueError('Interpreter discovery not supported for {0}'.format(interpreter_name))\n\n    host = task_vars.get('inventory_hostname', 'unknown')\n    res = None\n    platform_type = 'unknown'\n    found_interpreters = [u'/usr/bin/python']  # fallback value\n    is_auto_legacy = discovery_mode.startswith('auto_legacy')\n    is_silent = discovery_mode.endswith('_silent')\n\n    try:\n        platform_python_map = C.config.get_config_value('INTERPRETER_PYTHON_DISTRO_MAP', variables=task_vars)\n        bootstrap_python_list = C.config.get_config_value('INTERPRETER_PYTHON_FALLBACK', variables=task_vars)\n\n        display.vvv(msg=u\"Attempting {0} interpreter discovery\".format(interpreter_name), host=host)\n\n        # not all command -v impls accept a list of commands, so we have to call it once per python\n        command_list = [\"command -v '%s'\" % py for py in bootstrap_python_list]\n        shell_bootstrap = \"echo PLATFORM; uname; echo FOUND; {0}; echo ENDFOUND\".format('; '.join(command_list))\n\n        # FUTURE: in most cases we probably don't want to use become, but maybe sometimes we do?\n        res = action._low_level_execute_command(shell_bootstrap, sudoable=False)\n\n        raw_stdout = res.get('stdout', u'')\n\n        match = foundre.match(raw_stdout)\n\n        if not match:\n            display.debug(u'raw interpreter discovery output: {0}'.format(raw_stdout), host=host)\n            raise ValueError('unexpected output from Python interpreter discovery')\n\n        platform_type = match.groups()[0].lower().strip()\n\n        found_interpreters = [interp.strip() for interp in match.groups()[1].splitlines() if interp.startswith('/')]\n\n        display.debug(u\"found interpreters: {0}\".format(found_interpreters), host=host)\n\n        if not found_interpreters:\n            action._discovery_warnings.append(u'No python interpreters found for host {0} (tried {1})'.format(host, bootstrap_python_list))\n            # this is lame, but returning None or throwing an exception is uglier\n            return u'/usr/bin/python'\n\n        if platform_type != 'linux':\n            raise NotImplementedError('unsupported platform for extended discovery: {0}'.format(to_native(platform_type)))\n\n        platform_script = pkgutil.get_data('ansible.executor.discovery', 'python_target.py')\n\n        # FUTURE: respect pipelining setting instead of just if the connection supports it?\n        if action._connection.has_pipelining:\n            res = action._low_level_execute_command(found_interpreters[0], sudoable=False, in_data=platform_script)\n        else:\n            # FUTURE: implement on-disk case (via script action or ?)\n            raise NotImplementedError('pipelining support required for extended interpreter discovery')\n","numLines":100,"startLine":1,"totalLines":204}}}

First, we use a simple shell-agnostic bootstrap to\n    40→    # get the system type from uname, and find any random Python that can get us the info we need. For supported\n    41→    # target OS types, we'll dispatch a Python script that calls plaform.dist() (for older platforms, where available)\n    42→    # and brings back /etc/os-release (if present). The proper Python path is looked up in a table of known\n    43→    # distros/versions with included Pythons; if nothing is found, depending on the discovery mode, either the\n    44→    # default fallback of /usr/bin/python is used (if we know it's there), or discovery fails.\n    45→\n    46→    # FUTURE: add logical equivalence for \"python3\" in the case of py3-only modules?\n    47→    if interpreter_name != 'python':\n    48→        raise ValueError('Interpreter discovery not supported for {0}'.format(interpreter_name))\n    49→\n    50→    host = task_vars.get('inventory_hostname', 'unknown')\n    51→    res = None\n    52→    platform_type = 'unknown'\n    53→    found_interpreters = [u'/usr/bin/python']  # fallback value\n    54→    is_auto_legacy = discovery_mode.startswith('auto_legacy')\n    55→    is_silent = discovery_mode.endswith('_silent')\n    56→\n    57→    try:\n    58→        platform_python_map = C.config.get_config_value('INTERPRETER_PYTHON_DISTRO_MAP', variables=task_vars)\n    59→        bootstrap_python_list = C.config.get_config_value('INTERPRETER_PYTHON_FALLBACK', variables=task_vars)\n    60→\n    61→        display.vvv(msg=u\"Attempting {0} interpreter discovery\".format(interpreter_name), host=host)\n    62→\n    63→        # not all command -v impls accept a list of commands, so we have to call it once per python\n    64→        command_list = [\"command -v '%s'\" % py for py in bootstrap_python_list]\n    65→        shell_bootstrap = \"echo PLATFORM; uname; echo FOUND; {0}; echo ENDFOUND\".format('; '.join(command_list))\n    66→\n    67→        # FUTURE: in most cases we probably don't want to use become, but maybe sometimes we do?\n    68→        res = action._low_level_execute_command(shell_bootstrap, sudoable=False)\n    69→\n    70→        raw_stdout = res.get('stdout', u'')\n    71→\n    72→        match = foundre.match(raw_stdout)\n    73→\n    74→        if not match:\n    75→            display.debug(u'raw interpreter discovery output: {0}'.format(raw_stdout), host=host)\n    76→            raise ValueError('unexpected output from Python interpreter discovery')\n    77→\n    78→        platform_type = match.groups()[0].lower().strip()\n    79→\n    80→        found_interpreters = [interp.strip() for interp in match.groups()[1].splitlines() if interp.startswith('/')]\n    81→\n    82→        display.debug(u\"found interpreters: {0}\".format(found_interpreters), host=host)\n    83→\n    84→        if not found_interpreters:\n    85→            action._discovery_warnings.append(u'No python interpreters found for host {0} (tried {1})'.format(host, bootstrap_python_list))\n    86→            # this is lame, but returning None or throwing an exception is uglier\n    87→            return u'/usr/bin/python'\n    88→\n    89→        if platform_type != 'linux':\n    90→            raise NotImplementedError('unsupported platform for extended discovery: {0}'.format(to_native(platform_type)))\n    91→\n    92→        platform_script = pkgutil.get_data('ansible.executor.discovery', 'python_target.py')\n    93→\n    94→        # FUTURE: respect pipelining setting instead of just if the connection supports it?\n    95→        if action._connection.has_pipelining:\n    96→            res = action._low_level_execute_command(found_interpreters[0], sudoable=False, in_data=platform_script)\n    97→        else:\n    98→            # FUTURE: implement on-disk case (via script action or ?)\n    99→            raise NotImplementedError('pipelining support required for extended interpreter discovery')\n   100→\n   101→        platform_info = json.loads(res.get('stdout'))\n   102→\n   103→        distro, version = _get_linux_distro(platform_info)\n   104→\n   105→        if not distro or not version:\n   106→            raise NotImplementedError('unable to get Linux distribution/version info')\n   107→\n   108→        version_map = platform_python_map.get(distro.lower().strip())\n   109→        if not version_map:\n   110→            raise NotImplementedError('unsupported Linux distribution: {0}'.format(distro))\n   111→\n   112→        platform_interpreter = to_text(_version_fuzzy_match(version, version_map), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n   113→\n   114→        # provide a transition period for hosts that were using /usr/bin/python previously (but shouldn't have been)\n   115→        if is_auto_legacy:\n   116→            if platform_interpreter != u'/usr/bin/python' and u'/usr/bin/python' in found_interpreters:\n   117→                # FIXME: support comments in sivel's deprecation scanner so we can get reminded on this\n   118→                if not is_silent:\n   119→                    action._discovery_deprecation_warnings.append(dict(\n   120→                        msg=u\"Distribution {0} {1} on host {2} should use {3}, but is using \"\n   121→                            u\"/usr/bin/python for backward compatibility with prior Ansible releases. \"\n   122→                            u\"A future Ansible release will default to using the discovered platform \"\n   123→                            u\"python for this host. See {4} for more information\"\n   124→                            .format(distro, version, host, platform_interpreter,\n   125→                                    get_versioned_doclink('reference_appendices/interpreter_discovery.html')),\n   126→                        version='2.12'))\n   127→                return u'/usr/bin/python'\n   128→\n   129→        if platform_interpreter not in found_interpreters:\n   130→            if platform_interpreter not in bootstrap_python_list:\n   131→                # sanity check to make sure we looked for it\n   132→                if not is_silent:\n   133→                    action._discovery_warnings \\\n   134→                        .append(u\"Platform interpreter {0} on host {1} is missing from bootstrap list\"\n   135→                                .format(platform_interpreter, host))\n   136→\n   137→            if not is_silent:\n   138→                action._discovery_warnings \\\n   139→                    .append(u\"Distribution {0} {1} on host {2} should use {3}, but is using {4}, since the \"\n   140→                            u\"discovered platform python interpreter was not present. See {5} \"\n   141→                            u\"for more information.\"\n   142→                            .format(distro, version, host, platform_interpreter, found_interpreters[0],\n   143→                                    get_versioned_doclink('reference_appendices/interpreter_discovery.html')))\n   144→            return found_interpreters[0]\n   145→\n   146→        return platform_interpreter\n   147→    except NotImplementedError as ex:\n   148→        display.vvv(msg=u'Python interpreter discovery fallback ({0})'.format(to_text(ex)), host=host)\n   149→    except Exception as ex:\n   150→        if not is_silent:\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. It's only used for debugging on the\n   205→        # remote machine.\n   206→        #\n   207→        # The subcommands in this function make it easier to debug ansiballz\n   208→        # modules.  Here's the basic steps:\n   209→        #\n   210→        # Run ansible with the environment variable: ANSIBLE_KEEP_REMOTE_FILES=1 and -vvv\n   211→        # to save the module file remotely::\n   212→        #   $ ANSIBLE_KEEP_REMOTE_FILES=1 ansible host1 -m ping -a 'data=october' -vvv\n   213→        #\n   214→        # Part of the verbose output will tell you where on the remote machine the\n   215→        # module was written to::\n   216→        #   [...]\n   217→        #   <host1> SSH: EXEC ssh -C -q -o ControlMaster=auto -o ControlPersist=60s -o KbdInteractiveAuthentication=no -o\n   218→        #   PreferredAuthentications=gssapi-with-mic,gssapi-keyex,hostbased,publickey -o PasswordAuthentication=no -o ConnectTimeout=10 -o\n   219→        #   ControlPath=/home/badger/.ansible/cp/ansible-ssh-%%h-%%p-%%r -tt rhel7 '/bin/sh -c '\"'\"'LANG=en_US.UTF-8 LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8\n   220→        #   LC_MESSAGES=en_US.UTF-8 /usr/bin/python /home/badger/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1461173013.93-9076457629738/ping'\"'\"''\n   221→        #   [...]\n   222→        #\n   223→        # Login to the remote machine and run the module file via from the previous\n   224→        # step with the explode subcommand to extract the module payload into\n   225→        # source files::\n   226→        #   $ ssh host1\n   227→        #   $ /usr/bin/python /home/badger/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1461173013.93-9076457629738/ping explode\n   228→        #   Module expanded into:\n   229→        #   /home/badger/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1461173408.08-279692652635227/ansible\n   230→        #\n   231→        # You can now edit the source files to instrument the code or experiment with\n   232→        # different parameter values.  When you're ready to run the code you've modified\n   233→        # (instead of the code from the actual zipped module), use the execute subcommand like this::\n   234→        #   $ /usr/bin/python /home/badger/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1461173013.93-9076457629738/ping execute\n   235→\n   236→        # Okay to use __file__ here because we're running from a kept file\n   237→        basedir = os.path.join(os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__)), 'debug_dir')\n   238→        args_path = os.path.join(basedir, 'args')\n   239→\n   240→        if command == 'explode':\n   241→            # transform the ZIPDATA into an exploded directory of code and then\n   242→            # print the path to the code.  This is an easy way for people to look\n   243→            # at the code on the remote machine for debugging it in that\n   244→            # environment\n   245→            z = zipfile.ZipFile(zipped_mod)\n   246→            for filename in z.namelist():\n   247→                if filename.startswith('/'):\n   248→                    raise Exception('Something wrong with this module zip file: should not contain absolute paths')\n   249→\n   250→                dest_filename = os.path.join(basedir, filename)\n   251→                if dest_filename.endswith(os.path.sep) and not os.path.exists(dest_filename):\n   252→                    os.makedirs(dest_filename)\n   253→                else:\n   254→                    directory = os.path.dirname(dest_filename)\n   255→                    if not os.path.exists(directory):\n   256→                        os.makedirs(directory)\n   257→                    f = open(dest_filename, 'wb')\n   258→                    f.write(z.read(filename))\n   259→                    f.close()\n   260→\n   261→            # write the args file\n   262→            f = open(args_path, 'wb')\n   263→            f.write(json_params)\n   264→            f.close()\n   265→\n   266→            print('Module expanded into:')\n   267→            print('%%s' %% basedir)\n   268→            exitcode = 0\n   269→\n   270→        elif command == 'execute':\n   271→            # Execute the exploded code instead of executing the module from the\n   272→            # embedded ZIPDATA.  This allows people to easily run their modified\n   273→            # code on the remote machine to see how changes will affect it.\n   274→\n   275→            # Set pythonpath to the debug dir\n   276→            sys.path.insert(0, basedir)\n   277→\n   278→            # read in the args file which the user may have modified\n   279→            with open(args_path, 'rb') as f:\n   280→                json_params = f.read()\n   281→\n   282→            # Monkeypatch the parameters into basic\n   283→            from ansible.module_utils import basic\n   284→            basic._ANSIBLE_ARGS = json_params\n   285→\n   286→            # Run the module!  By importing it as '__main__', it thinks it is executing as a script\n   287→            runpy.run_module(mod_name='%(module_fqn)s', init_globals=None, run_name='__main__', alter_sys=True)\n   288→\n   289→            # Ansible modules must exit themselves\n   290→            print('{\"msg\": \"New-style module did not handle its own exit\", \"failed\": true}')\n   291→            sys.exit(1)\n   292→\n   293→        else:\n   294→            print('WARNING: Unknown debug command.  Doing nothing.')\n   295→            exitcode = 0\n   296→\n   297→        return exitcode\n   298→\n   299→    #\n   300→    # See comments in the debug() method for information on debugging\n   301→    #\n   302→\n   303→    ANSIBALLZ_PARAMS = %(params)s\n   304→    if PY3:\n   305→        ANSIBALLZ_PARAMS = ANSIBALLZ_PARAMS.encode('utf-8')\n   306→    try:\n   307→        # There's a race condition with the controller removing the\n   308→        # remote_tmpdir and this module executing under async.  So we cannot\n   309→        # store this in remote_tmpdir (use system tempdir instead)\n   310→        # Only need to use [ansible_module]_payload_ in the temp_path until we move to zipimport\n   311→        # (this helps ansible-test produce coverage stats)\n   312→        temp_path = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='ansible_%(ansible_module)s_payload_')\n   313→\n   314→        zipped_mod = os.path.join(temp_path, 'ansible_%(ansible_module)s_payload.zip')\n   315→        with open(zipped_mod, 'wb') as modlib:\n   316→            modlib.write(base64.b64decode(ZIPDATA))\n   317→\n   318→        if len(sys.argv) == 2:\n   319→            exitcode = debug(sys.argv[1], zipped_mod, ANSIBALLZ_PARAMS)\n   320→        else:\n   321→            # tempdir creation probably failed\n   328→            pass\n   329→    sys.exit(exitcode)\n   330→\n   331→if __name__ == '__main__':\n   332→    _ansiballz_main()\n   333→'''\n   334→\n   335→ANSIBALLZ_COVERAGE_TEMPLATE = '''\n   336→        # adjust soft limit subject to existing hard limit\n   384→    requested_soft = min(existing_hard, %(rlimit_nofile)d)\n   385→\n   386→    if requested_soft != existing_soft:\n   387→        try:\n   388→            resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE, (requested_soft, existing_hard))\n   389→        except ValueError:\n   390→            # {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01RnzGxubRpwhPcH2EASnzwG","type":"tool_result","content":"  1288→def modify_module(module_name, module_path, module_args, templar, task_vars=None, module_compression='ZIP_STORED', async_timeout=0, become=False,\n  1289→                  become_method=None, become_user=None, become_password=None, become_flags=None, environment=None):\n  1290→    \"\"\"\n  1291→    Used to insert chunks of code into modules before transfer rather than\n  1292→    doing regular python imports.  This allows for more efficient transfer in\n  1293→    a non-bootstrapping scenario by not moving extra files over the wire and\n  1294→    also takes care of embedding arguments in the transferred modules.\n  1295→\n  1296→    This version is done in such a way that local imports can still be\n  1297→    used in the module code, so IDEs don't have to be aware of what is going on.\n  1298→\n  1299→    Example:\n  1300→\n  1301→    from ansible.module_utils.basic import *\n  1302→\n  1303→       ... will result in the insertion of basic.py into the module\n  1304→       from the module_utils/ directory in the source tree.\n  1305→\n  1306→    For powershell, this code effectively no-ops, as the exec wrapper requires access to a number of\n  1307→    properties not available here.\n  1308→\n  1309→    \"\"\"\n  1310→    task_vars = {} if task_vars is None else task_vars\n  1311→    environment = {} if environment is None else environment\n  1312→\n  1313→    with open(module_path, 'rb') as f:\n  1314→\n  1315→        # Yum4 is a yum\n    37→        command-line compatibility layer on top of dnf. Since the Ansible\n    38→        modules for yum(aka yum3) and dnf(aka yum4) call each of yum3 and yum4's\n    39→        python APIs natively on the backend, we need to handle this here and\n    40→        pass off to the correct Ansible module to execute on the remote system.\n    41→        '''\n    42→\n    43→        self._supports_check_mode = True\n    44→        self._supports_async = True\n    45→\n    46→        result = super(ActionModule, self).run(tmp, task_vars)\n    47→        del tmp  # tmp no longer has any effect\n    48→\n    49→        # Carry-over concept from the package action plugin\n    50→        module = self._task.args.get('use_backend', \"auto\")\n    51→\n    52→        if module == 'auto':\n    53→            try:\n    54→                if self._task.delegate_to:  # if we delegate, we should use delegated host's facts\n    55→                    module = self._templar.template(\"{{hostvars['%s']['ansible_facts']['pkg_mgr']}}\" % self._task.delegate_to)\n    56→                else:\n    57→                    module = self._templar.template(\"{{ansible_facts.pkg_mgr}}\")\n    58→            except Exception:\n    59→                pass  # could not get it from template!\n    60→\n    61→        if module not in VALID_BACKENDS:\n    62→            facts = self._execute_module(\n    63→                module_name=\"ansible.legacy.setup\", module_args=dict(filter=\"ansible_pkg_mgr\", gather_subset=\"!all\"),\n    64→                task_vars=task_vars)\n    65→            display.debug(\"Facts %s\" % facts)\n    66→            module = facts.get(\"ansible_facts\", {}).get(\"ansible_pkg_mgr\", \"auto\")\n    67→            if (not self._task.delegate_to or self._task.delegate_facts) and module != 'auto':\n    68→                result['ansible_facts'] = {'pkg_mgr': module}\n    69→\n    70→        if module not in VALID_BACKENDS:\n    71→            result.update(\n    72→                {\n    73→                    'failed': True,\n    74→                    'msg': (\"Could not detect which major revision of yum is in use, which is required to determine module backend.\",\n    75→                            \"You should manually specify use_backend to tell the module whether to use the yum (yum3) or dnf (yum4) backend})\"),\n    76→                }\n    77→            )\n    78→\n    79→        else:\n    80→            if module == \"yum4\":\n    81→                module = \"dnf\"\n    82→\n    83→            # eliminate collisions with collections search while still allowing local override\n    84→            module = 'ansible.legacy.' + module\n    85→\n    86→            if not self._shared_loader_obj.module_loader.has_plugin(module):\n    87→                result.update({'failed': True, 'msg': \"Could not find a yum module backend for %s.\" % module})\n    88→            else:\n    89→                # Cleanup\n    99→        if not self._task.async_val:\n   100→            # remove a temporary path we created\n   101→            self._remove_tmp_path(self._connection._shell.tmpdir)\n   102→\n   103→        return result\n   104→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 447→def _sanitize_keys_conditions(value, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals):\n   448→    \"\"\" Helper method to sanitize_keys() to build deferred_removals and avoid deep recursion. removing no_log values from key names.\n   487→\n   488→    This is a companion function to the `remove_values()` function. Similar to that function,\n   489→    we make use of deferred_removals to avoid hitting maximum recursion depth in cases of\n   490→    large data structures.\n   491→\n   492→    :param obj: The container object to sanitize. Currently filters:\n   532→    # user:pass@foo/whatever and http://username:pass@wherever/foo\n   533→    # This code has false positives and consumes parts of logs that are\n   534→    # not passwds\n   535→\n   536→    # begin: start of a passwd containing string\n   537→    # end: end of a passwd containing string\n   538→    # sep: char between user and passwd\n   539→    # prev_begin: where in the overall string to start a search for\n   540→    #   a passwd\n   541→    # sep_search_end: where in the string to end a search for the sep\n   542→    data = to_native(data)\n   543→\n   544→    output = []\n   545→    begin = len(data)\n   546→    prev_begin = begin\n   547→    sep = 1\n   548→    while sep:\n   549→        # Find the potential end of a passwd\n   550→        try:\n   551→            end = data.rindex('@', 0, begin)\n   552→        except ValueError:\n   553→            # No passwd in the rest of the data\n   554→            output.insert(0, data[0:begin])\n   555→            break\n   556→\n   557→        # Search for the beginning of a passwd\n   558→        sep = None\n   559→        sep_search_end = end\n   560→        while not sep:\n   561→            # URL-style username+password\n   562→            try:\n   563→                begin = data.rindex('://', 0, sep_search_end)\n   564→            except ValueError:\n   565→                # No url style in the data, check for ssh style in the\n   566→                # rest of the string\n   567→                begin = 0\n   568→            # Search for separator\n   569→            try:\n   570→                sep = data.index(':', begin + 3, end)\n   571→            except ValueError:\n   572→                # No separator; choices:\n   573→                if begin == 0:\n   574→                    # Searched the whole string so there's no password\n   575→                    # here.  Return the remaining data\n   576→                    output.insert(0, data[0:begin])\n   577→                    break\n   578→                # Search for a different beginning of the password field.\n   579→                sep_search_end = begin\n   580→                continue\n   581→        if sep:\n   582→            # Password was found; remove it.\n   583→            output.insert(0, data[end:prev_begin])\n   584→            output.insert(0, '********')\n   585→            output.insert(0, data[begin:sep + 1])\n   586→            prev_begin = begin\n   587→\n   588→    output = ''.join(output)\n   589→    if no_log_values:\n   590→        output = remove_values(output, no_log_values)\n   591→    return output\n   592→\n   593→\n   594→def _load_params():\n   595→    ''' read the modules parameters and store them globally.\n   596→\n   597→    This function may be needed for certain very dynamic custom modules which\n   598→    want to process the parameters that are being handed the module.  Since\n   599→    this is so closely tied to the implementation of modules we cannot\n   600→    guarantee API stability for it (it may change between versions) however we\n   601→    will try not to break it gratuitously.  It is certainly more future-proof\n   602→    to call this function and consume its outputs than to implement the logic\n   603→    inside it as a copy in your own code.\n   604→    '''\n   605→    global _ANSIBLE_ARGS\n   606→    if _ANSIBLE_ARGS is not None:\n   607→        buffer = _ANSIBLE_ARGS\n   608→    else:\n   609→        # debug overrides to read args from file or cmdline\n   610→\n   611→        # Avoid tracebacks when locale is non-utf8\n   612→        # We control the args and we pass them as utf8\n   613→        if len(sys.argv) > 1:\n   614→            if os.path.isfile(sys.argv[1]):\n   615→                fd = open(sys.argv[1], 'rb')\n   616→                buffer = fd.read()\n   617→                fd.close()\n   618→            else:\n   619→                buffer = sys.argv[1]\n   620→                if PY3:\n   621→                    buffer = buffer.encode('utf-8', errors='surrogateescape')\n   622→        # default case, read from stdin\n   623→        else:\n   624→            if PY2:\n   625→                buffer = sys.stdin.read()\n   626→            else:\n   627→                buffer = sys.stdin.buffer.read()\n   628→        _ANSIBLE_ARGS = buffer\n   629→\n   630→    try:\n   631→        params = json.loads(buffer.decode('utf-8'))\n   632→    except ValueError:\n   633→        # This helper used too early for fail_json to work.\n   634→        print('\\n{\"msg\": \"Error: Module unable to decode valid JSON on stdin.  Unable to figure out what parameters were passed\", \"failed\": true}')\n   635→        sys.exit(1)\n   636→\n   637→    if PY2:\n   638→        params = json_dict_unicode_to_bytes(params)\n   639→\n   640→    try:\n   641→        return params['ANSIBLE_MODULE_ARGS']\n   642→    except KeyError:\n   643→        # This helper does not have access to fail_json so we have to print\n   644→        # json output on our own.\n   645→        print('\\n{\"msg\": \"Error: Module unable to locate ANSIBLE_MODULE_ARGS in json data from stdin.  Unable to figure out what parameters were passed\", '\n   646→              '\"failed\": true}')\n   647→        sys.exit(1)\n   648→\n   649→\n   650→def missing_required_lib(library, reason=None, url=None):\n   651→    hostname = platform.node()\n   652→    msg = \"Failed to import the required Python library (%s) on %s's Python %s.\" % (library, hostname, sys.executable)\n   653→    if reason:\n   654→        msg += \" This is required %s.\" % reason\n   655→    if url:\n   656→        msg += \" See %s for more info.\" % url\n   657→\n   658→    msg += (\" Please read the module documentation and install it in the appropriate location.\"\n   659→            \" If the required library is installed, but Ansible is using the wrong Python interpreter,\"\n   660→            \" please consult the documentation on ansible_python_interpreter\")\n   661→    return msg\n   662→\n   663→\n   664→class AnsibleFallbackNotFound(Exception):\n   665→    pass\n   666→\n   667→\n   668→class AnsibleModule(object):\n   669→    def __init__(self, argument_spec, bypass_checks=False, no_log=False,\n   670→                 mutually_exclusive=None, required_together=None,\n   671→                 required_one_of=None, add_file_common_args=False,\n   672→                 supports_check_mode=False, required_if=None, required_by=None):\n   673→\n   674→        '''\n   675→        Common code for quickly building an ansible module in Python\n   676→        (although you can write modules with anything that can return JSON).\n   677→\n   678→        See :ref:`developing_modules_general` for a general introduction\n   679→        and :ref:`developing_program_flow_modules` for more detailed explanation.\n   680→        '''\n   681→\n   682→        self._name = os.path.basename(__file__)  # http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/extensions/autodoc.html#confval-autodoc_docstring_signature\n    71→    '''is_executable(path)\n    72→\n    73→    is the given path executable?\n    74→\n    75→    :arg path: The path of the file to check.\n    76→\n    77→    Limitations:\n    78→\n    79→    * Does not account for FSACLs.\n    80→    * Most times we really want to know \"Can the current user execute this\n    81→      file\".  This function does not tell us that, only if any execute bit is set.\n    82→    '''\n    83→    # These are all bitfields so first bitwise-or all the permissions we're\n    84→    # looking for, then bitwise-and with the file's mode to determine if any\n    85→    # execute bits are set.\n    86→    return ((stat.S_IXUSR | stat.S_IXGRP | stat.S_IXOTH) & os.stat(path)[stat.ST_MODE])\n    87→\n    88→\n    89→def format_attributes(attributes):\n    90→    attribute_list = [FILE_ATTRIBUTES.get(attr) for attr in attributes if attr in FILE_ATTRIBUTES]\n    91→    return attribute_list\n    92→\n    93→\n    94→def get_flags_from_attributes(attributes):\n    95→    flags = [key for key, attr in FILE_ATTRIBUTES.items() if attr in attributes]\n    96→    return ''.join(flags)\n    97→\n    98→\n    99→def get_file_arg_spec():\n   100→    arg_spec = dict(\n   101→        mode=dict(type='raw'),\n   102→        owner=dict(),\n   103→        group=dict(),\n   104→        seuser=dict(),\n   105→        serole=dict(),\n   106→        selevel=dict(),\n   107→        setype=dict(),\n   108→        attributes=dict(aliases=['attr']),\n   109→    )\n   110→    return arg_spec\n   111→\n   112→\n   113→class LockTimeout(Exception):\n   114→    pass\n   115→\n   116→\n   117→class FileLock:\n   118→    '''\n   119→    Currently FileLock is implemented via fcntl.flock on a lock file, however this\n   120→    behaviour may change in the future. Avoid mixing lock types fcntl.flock,\n   121→    fcntl.lockf and module_utils.common.file.FileLock as it will certainly cause\n   122→    unwanted and/or unexpected behaviour\n   123→    '''\n   124→    def __init__(self):\n   125→        self.lockfd = None\n   126→\n   127→    @contextmanager\n   128→    def lock_file(self, path, tmpdir, lock_timeout=None):\n   129→        '''\n   130→        Context for lock acquisition\n   131→        '''\n   132→        try:\n   133→            self.set_lock(path, tmpdir, lock_timeout)\n   134→            yield\n   135→        finally:\n   136→            self.unlock()\n   137→\n   138→    def set_lock(self, path, tmpdir, lock_timeout=None):\n   139→        '''\n   140→        Create a lock file based on path with flock to prevent other processes\n   141→        using given path.\n   142→        Please note that currently file locking only works when it's executed by\n   143→        the same user, I.E single user scenarios\n   144→\n   145→        :kw path: Path (file) to lock\n   146→        :kw tmpdir: Path where to place the temporary .lock file\n   147→        :kw lock_timeout:\n   148→            Wait n seconds for lock acquisition, fail if timeout is reached.\n   149→            0 = Do not wait, fail if lock cannot be acquired immediately,\n   150→            Default is None, wait indefinitely until lock is released.\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01LpEgYs3XfCvb59TauR91pr","session_id":"bec4cbd0-2fe3-47f4-a8b9-5164d7885cf9","uuid":"ddc8a385-ff47-4bcb-b486-da9047029091"}

SELINUX HANDLING IN MODULES\n\n**Current SELinux Implementation:**\n\n1. **SELinux Import Pattern** (`module_utils/basic.py`, line 75-80):\n   ```python\n   HAVE_SELINUX = False\n   try:\n       import selinux\n       HAVE_SELINUX = True\n   except ImportError:\n       pass\n   ```\n\n2. **SELinux Detection** (`module_utils/facts/system/selinux.py`, line 23-27):\n   - Attempts to import selinux library\n   - Sets `HAVE_SELINUX` flag\n   - Gracefully handles missing library with facts `selinux_python_present = False`\n\n3. **SELinux Context Operations** (`module_utils/basic.py`):\n   - `selinux_enabled()` (line 879-924): Checks if SELinux is enabled\n   - `selinux_context()` (line 988-1010): Gets file SELinux context\n   - `set_context_if_different()` (line 1463+): Sets SELinux context on files\n   - `is_special_selinux_path()` (line 909-923): Detects special filesystems\n\n4. **SELinux Special Filesystems** (`module_common.py`, line 76, 1277):\n   - Defined as: `REPLACER_SELINUX = b\"<<SELINUX_SPECIAL_FILESYSTEMS>>\"`\n   - Replaced during module build (line 1277): `b_module_data.replace(REPLACER_SELINUX, to_bytes(','.join(C.DEFAULT_SELINUX_SPECIAL_FS)))`\n   - Config: `/workspace/lib/ansible/config/base.yml` (line 1104): `DEFAULT_SELINUX_SPECIAL_FS`\n\n5. **SELinux Operations Without libselinux-python:**\n   - Tests show fallback pattern (`test_selinux.py`, line 68-75): When `HAVE_SELINUX = False`, calls `selinuxenabled` command\n   - If binary exits with rc=0, the system has SELinux but library missing\n   - Current behavior: raises SystemExit when library needed but not available\n\n### 4. DEPENDENCY HANDLING IN PACKAGE MANAGERS\n\n**APT Module** (`modules/apt_repository.py`, lines 81-96):\n- Documentation lists requirements: `python-apt` (Python 2), `python3-apt` (Python 3)\n- Parameter `install_python_apt` (default: true) auto-installs missing library\n- Uses `run_command()` to execute apt-get install if needed\n\n**YUM Module** (`modules/yum.py`, lines 1-150):\n- Uses native Python yum/dnf APIs\n- Action plugin (`plugins/action/yum.py`, lines 28-103) routes to appropriate backend\n- Detects yum vs dnf via `ansible_pkg_mgr` fact\n- Falls back to setup module if needed (line 62-66)\n\n**DNF Module** (`modules/dnf.py`, lines 1-100):\n- Similar to yum, uses native API bindings\n\n**Missing Dependency Detection Pattern:**\n- Try import → ImportError caught → fallback mechanism or fail_json\n- `missing_required_lib()` utility (`module_utils/basic.py`, line 650-661) provides standard error message\n\n### 5. The `_execute_module` method calls `_configure_module` to get the module details, then executes it via `_low_level_execute_command`, and finally parses the result with `_parse_returned_data`.\n\nFor respawn to work, I need to:\n\n1. Create a way for modules to indicate they need to respawn\n2. Detect this condition after module execution\n3. Find a compatible interpreter that has the required dependencies\n4. Re-execute the module with that interpreter\n\nThe issue is that unlike interpreter discovery (which happens during configure), respawn needs to happen after we know what the module requires. So the flow would be:\n\n1. Execute module with current interpreter\n2. Module fails because it's missing a dependency\n3. Module detects missing dependency and returns a special error/status\n4. Action plugin detects this special status\n5. Action plugin finds a compatible interpreter that has the dependency\n6. This causes the module to fail completely.\n\nNow, I need to implement a respawn mechanism. Let me think about the approach:\n\n1. **Option 1: Detect missing dependency early and signal respawn**\n   - Modules detect missing dependency at startup\n   - Signal a special error code/message for respawn\n   - Action plugin catches this and retries with different interpreter\n\n2. **Option 2: Detect missing dependencies during configure_module**\n   - Extend the modify_module process to validate dependencies\n   - Similar to interpreter discovery\n   - Raise a new error type like `ModuleRespawnRequired`\n\nOption 1 is more practical because:- Modules already have the logic to check for missing dependencies\n- We just need to change what they do when they detect a missing dependency\n- The action plugin can detect this after execution and retry\n\nLet me design the approach:\n\n1. They can't raise exceptions to the action plugin. So the mechanism needs to be:\n\n1. Module detects missing dependency\n2. Module returns a special JSON response with a respawn flag\n3. Action plugin detects this flag\n4. Action plugin finds compatible interpreter\n5. Action plugin re-executes module\n\nBut wait, if the module can't even run because of missing dependencies (e.g., can't import the module), then it can't return proper JSON. So I need a different approach.\n\nLet me think about this more carefully. The issue says \"modules such as dnf, yum, apt, apt_repository, and others currently rely on system-specific Python bindings that may not be available\". \n\nLooking at the dnf module, it attempts to import dnf first, and if that fails it tries to install it, only calling fail_json if installation also fails. So the module does execute even when the binding isn't initially present. The real problem emerges when using a non-system Python interpreter like a virtualenv or conda environment—the module runs in that interpreter, but the system package manager installs to a different location. The respawn mechanism should handle this by detecting when a required system binding is missing and signaling that a different interpreter is needed.\n\nThe approach involves having the module indicate it needs to run under a system interpreter through a special JSON response flag. The action plugin would then detect this signal and retry execution using the system Python interpreter instead. To implement this, I need to add a response key that modules can use to request respawning, update the data parsing logic to recognize this signal, add retry logic to the module execution that attempts the module again with an alternative interpreter, create a helper to identify compatible system interpreters, and update the package manager modules to leverage this mechanism. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_011opbBakH2UjXhZwtr81Kha","type":"tool_result","content":"# Copyright: (c) 2021, Ansible Project\n# GNU General Public License v3.0+ (see COPYING or https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0.txt)\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport os\nimport pathlib\nimport subprocess\nimport sys\nimport typing as t\n\nfrom ansible.module_utils.common.text.converters import to_bytes\n\n_ANSIBLE_PARENT_PATH = pathlib.Path(__file__).parents[3]\n\n\ndef has_respawned():\n    return hasattr(sys.modules['__main__'], '_respawned')\n\n\ndef respawn_module(interpreter_path) -> t.NoReturn:\n    \"\"\"\n    Respawn the currently-running Ansible Python module under the specified Python interpreter.\n\n    Ansible modules that require libraries that are typically available only under well-known interpreters\n    (eg, ``apt``, ``dnf``) can use bespoke logic to determine the libraries they need are not\n    available, then call `respawn_module` to re-execute the current module under a different interpreter\n    and exit the current process when the new subprocess has completed. The respawned process inherits only\n    stdout/stderr from the current process.\n\n    Only a single respawn is allowed. ``respawn_module`` will fail on nested respawns. Modules are encouraged\n    to call `has_respawned()` to defensively guide behavior before calling ``respawn_module``, and to ensure\n    that the target interpreter exists, as ``respawn_module`` will not fail gracefully.\n\n    :arg interpreter_path: path to a Python interpreter to respawn the current module\n    \"\"\"\n\n    if has_respawned():\n        raise Exception('module has already been respawned')\n\n    # FUTURE: we need a safe way to log that a respawn has occurred for forensic/debug purposes\n    payload = _create_payload()\n    stdin_read, stdin_write = os.pipe()\n    os.write(stdin_write, to_bytes(payload))\n    os.close(stdin_write)\n    rc = subprocess.call([interpreter_path, '--'], stdin=stdin_read)\n    sys.exit(rc)  # pylint: disable=ansible-bad-function\n\n\ndef probe_interpreters_for_module(interpreter_paths, module_name):\n    \"\"\"\n    Probes a supplied list of Python interpreters, returning the first one capable of\n    importing the named module. This is useful when attempting to locate a \"system\n    Python\" where OS-packaged utility modules are located.\n\n    :arg interpreter_paths: iterable of paths to Python interpreters. The paths will be probed\n    in order, and the first path that exists and can successfully import the named module will\n    be returned (or ``None`` if probing fails for all supplied paths).\n    :arg module_name: fully-qualified Python module name to probe for (for example, ``selinux``)\n    \"\"\"\n    PYTHONPATH = os.getenv('PYTHONPATH', '')\n    env = os.environ.copy()\n    env.update({\n        'PYTHONPATH': f'{_ANSIBLE_PARENT_PATH}:{PYTHONPATH}'.rstrip(': ')\n    })\n    for interpreter_path in interpreter_paths:\n        if not os.path.exists(interpreter_path):\n            continue\n        try:\n            rc = subprocess.call(\n                [\n                    interpreter_path,\n                    '-c',\n                    f'import {module_name}, ansible.module_utils.basic',\n                ],\n                env=env,\n            )\n            if rc == 0:\n                return interpreter_path\n        except Exception:\n            continue\n\n    return None\n\n\ndef _create_payload():\n    from ansible.module_utils import basic\n    smuggled_args = getattr(basic, '_ANSIBLE_ARGS')\n    if not smuggled_args:\n        raise Exception('unable to access ansible.module_utils.basic._ANSIBLE_ARGS (not launched by AnsiballZ?)')\n    module_fqn = sys.modules['__main__']._module_fqn\n    modlib_path = Python interpreter.\n\n    Ansible modules that require libraries that are typically available only under well-known interpreters\n    (eg, ``apt``, ``dnf``) can use bespoke logic to determine the libraries they need are not\n    available, then call `respawn_module` to re-execute the current module under a different interpreter\n    and exit the current process when the new subprocess has completed. The respawned process inherits only\n    stdout/stderr from the current process.\n\n    Only a single respawn is allowed. ``respawn_module`` will fail on nested respawns. Modules are encouraged\n    to call `has_respawned()` to defensively guide behavior before calling ``respawn_module``, and to ensure\n    that the target interpreter exists, as ``respawn_module`` will not fail gracefully.\n\n    :arg interpreter_path: path to a Python interpreter to respawn the current module\n    \"\"\"\n\n    if has_respawned():\n        raise Exception('module has already been respawned')\n\n    # FUTURE: we need a safe way to log that a respawn has occurred for forensic/debug purposes\n    payload = _create_payload()\n    stdin_read, stdin_write = os.pipe()\n    os.write(stdin_write, to_bytes(payload))\n    os.close(stdin_write)\n    rc = subprocess.call([interpreter_path, '--'], stdin=stdin_read)\n    sys.exit(rc)  # pylint: disable=ansible-bad-function\n\n\ndef probe_interpreters_for_module(interpreter_paths, module_name):\n    \"\"\"\n    Probes a supplied list of Python interpreters, returning the first one capable of\n    importing the named module.       'PYTHONPATH': f'{_ANSIBLE_PARENT_PATH}:{PYTHONPATH}'.rstrip(': ')\n    })\n    for interpreter_path in interpreter_paths:\n        if not os.path.exists(interpreter_path):\n ansible.module_utils.basic',\n                ],\n                env=env,\n            )\n            if rc == 0:\n                return interpreter_path\n        except Exception:\n {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01WLoQ2iiUPvwKh5wqnAhW7C","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_019wzVwufFyqefqZ5zRLptk6","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/workspace/lib/ansible/module_utils/common/respawn.py","content":"# Copyright: (c) 2021, Ansible Project\n# GNU General Public License v3.0+ (see COPYING or https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0.txt)\n\nfrom __future__ import absolute_import, division, print_function\n__metaclass__ = type\n\nimport os\nimport pathlib\nimport subprocess\nimport sys\n\nfrom ansible.module_utils.common.text.converters import to_bytes\n\n_ANSIBLE_PARENT_PATH = pathlib.Path(__file__).parents[3]\n\n\ndef has_respawned():\n    \"\"\"Check if the current module has already been respawned.\"\"\"\n    return hasattr(sys.modules['__main__'], '_respawned')\n\n\ndef respawn_module(interpreter_path):\n    \"\"\"\n    Respawn the currently-running Ansible Python module under the specified Python interpreter.\n\n    Ansible modules that require libraries that are typically available only under well-known interpreters\n    (e.g., ``apt``, ``dnf``) can use bespoke logic to determine the libraries they need are not\n    available, then call `respawn_module` to re-execute the current module under a different interpreter\n    and exit the current process when the new subprocess has completed. The respawned process inherits only\n    stdout/stderr from the current process.\n\n    Only a single respawn is allowed. ``respawn_module`` will fail on nested respawns. Modules are encouraged\n    to call `has_respawned()` to defensively guide behavior before calling ``respawn_module``, and to ensure\n    that the target interpreter exists, as ``respawn_module`` will not fail gracefully.\n\n    :arg interpreter_path: path to a Python interpreter to respawn the current module\n    \"\"\"\n\n    if has_respawned():\n        raise Exception('module has already been respawned')\n\n    # FUTURE: we need a safe way to log that a respawn has occurred for forensic/debug purposes\n    payload = _create_payload()\n    stdin_read, stdin_write = os.pipe()\n    os.write(stdin_write, to_bytes(payload))\n    os.close(stdin_write)\n    rc = subprocess.call([interpreter_path, '--'], stdin=stdin_read)\n    sys.exit(rc)  # pylint: disable=ansible-bad-function\n\n\ndef probe_interpreters_for_module(interpreter_paths, module_name):\n    \"\"\"\n    Probes a supplied list of Python interpreters, returning the first one capable of\n    importing the named module. This is useful when attempting to locate a \"system\n    Python\" where OS-packaged utility modules are located.\n\n    :arg interpreter_paths: iterable of paths to Python interpreters. The paths will be probed\n    in order, and the first path that exists and can successfully import the named module will\n    be returned (or ``None`` if probing fails for all supplied paths).\n    :arg module_name: fully-qualified Python module name to probe for (e.g., ``selinux``)\n    \"\"\"\n    PYTHONPATH = os.getenv('PYTHONPATH', '')\n    env = os.environ.copy()\n    env['PYTHONPATH'] = '{0}:{1}'.format(_ANSIBLE_PARENT_PATH, PYTHONPATH).rstrip(': ')\n\n    for interpreter_path in interpreter_paths:\n        if not os.path.exists(interpreter_path):\n            continue\n        try:\n            rc = subprocess.call(\n                [\n                    interpreter_path,\n                    '-c',\n                    'import {0}, ansible.module_utils.basic'.format(module_name),\n                ],\n                env=env,\n            )\n            if rc == 0:\n                return interpreter_path\n        except Exception:\n            continue\n\n    return None\n\n\ndef _create_payload():\n    \"\"\"Create the payload code for respawning a module.\"\"\"\n    from ansible.module_utils import basic\n    smuggled_args = getattr(basic, '_ANSIBLE_ARGS')\n    if not smuggled_args:\n        raise Exception('unable to access ansible.module_utils.basic._ANSIBLE_ARGS (not launched by AnsiballZ?)')\n\n    module_fqn = sys.modules['__main__']._module_fqn\n    modlib_path = sys.modules['__main__']._modlib_path\n\n    respawn_code_template = \"\"\"\nimport runpy\nimport sys\n\nmodule_fqn = {module_fqn!r}\nmodlib_path = {modlib_path!r}\nsmuggled_args = {smuggled_args!r}\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    sys.path.insert(0, modlib_path)\n\n    from ansible.module_utils import basic\n    basic._ANSIBLE_ARGS = smuggled_args\n\n    runpy.run_module(module_fqn, init_globals=dict(_respawned=True), run_name='__main__', alter_sys=True)\n    \"\"\"\n\n    respawn_code = respawn_code_template.format(\n        module_fqn=module_fqn,\n        modlib_path=modlib_path,\n        smuggled_args=smuggled_args.strip()\n    )\n\n    return respawn_code\n"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":661,"cache_read_input_tokens":62738,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":661},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"bec4cbd0-2fe3-47f4-a8b9-5164d7885cf9","uuid":"0e59e808-7f89-4893-8f48-ee331ea286d8"}

It is passed into\n        this function so that more entries can be added to it if value is\n        a container type.  The format of each entry is a 2-tuple where the first\n        element is the ``value`` parameter and the second value is a new\n        container to copy the elements of ``value`` into once iterated.\n    :returns: if ``value`` is a scalar, returns ``value`` with two exceptions:\n        1. :class:`~datetime.datetime` objects which are changed into a string representation.\n        2. objects which are in no_log_strings are replaced with a placeholder\n            so that no sensitive data is leaked.\n        If ``value`` is a container type, returns a new empty container.\n\n    ``deferred_removals`` is added to as a side-effect of this function.\n\n    .. warning:: It is up to the caller to make sure the order in which value\n        is passed in is correct.  For instance, higher level containers need\n        to be passed in before lower level containers. For example, given\n        ``{'level1': {'level2': 'level3': [True]} }`` first pass in the\n        dictionary for ``level1``, then the dict for ``level2``, and finally\n        the list for ``level3``.\n    \"\"\"\n    if isinstance(value, (text_type, binary_type)):\n        # Need native str type\n        native_str_value = value\n        if isinstance(value, text_type):\n            value_is_text = True\n            if PY2:\n                native_str_value = to_bytes(value, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n        elif isinstance(value, binary_type):\n            value_is_text = False\n            if PY3:\n                native_str_value = to_text(value, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n        if native_str_value in no_log_strings:\n            return 'VALUE_SPECIFIED_IN_NO_LOG_PARAMETER'\n        for omit_me in no_log_strings:\n            native_str_value = native_str_value.replace(omit_me, '*' * 8)\n\n        if value_is_text and isinstance(native_str_value, binary_type):\n            value = to_text(native_str_value, encoding='utf-8', errors='surrogate_then_replace')\n        elif not value_is_text and isinstance(native_str_value, text_type):\n            value = to_bytes(native_str_value, encoding='utf-8', errors='surrogate_then_replace')\n        else:\n            value = native_str_value\n\n    elif isinstance(value, Sequence):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableSequence):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = []  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        value = new_value\n\n    elif isinstance(value, Set):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableSet):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = set()  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        value = new_value\n\n    elif isinstance(value, Mapping):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableMapping):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = {}  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        value = new_value\n\n    elif isinstance(value, tuple(chain(integer_types, (float, bool, NoneType)))):\n        stringy_value = to_native(value, encoding='utf-8', errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n        if stringy_value in no_log_strings:\n            return 'VALUE_SPECIFIED_IN_NO_LOG_PARAMETER'\n        for omit_me in no_log_strings:\n            if omit_me in stringy_value:\n                return 'VALUE_SPECIFIED_IN_NO_LOG_PARAMETER'\n\n    elif isinstance(value, (datetime.datetime, datetime.date)):\n        value = value.isoformat()\n    else:\n        raise TypeError('Value of unknown type: %s, %s' % (type(value), value))\n\n    return value\n\n\ndef remove_values(value, no_log_strings):\n    \"\"\" Remove strings in no_log_strings from value.  If value is a container\n    type, then remove a lot more.\n\n    Use of deferred_removals exists, rather than a pure recursive solution,\n    because of the potential to hit the maximum recursion depth when dealing with\n    large amounts of data (see issue #24560).\n    \"\"\"\n\n    deferred_removals = deque()\n\n    no_log_strings = [to_native(s, errors='surrogate_or_strict') for s in no_log_strings]\n    new_value = _remove_values_conditions(value, no_log_strings, deferred_removals)\n\n    while deferred_removals:\n        old_data, new_data = deferred_removals.popleft()\n        if isinstance(new_data, Mapping):\n            for old_key, old_elem in old_data.items():\n                new_elem = _remove_values_conditions(old_elem, no_log_strings, deferred_removals)\n                new_data[old_key] = new_elem\n        else:\n            for elem in old_data:\n                new_elem = _remove_values_conditions(elem, no_log_strings, deferred_removals)\n                if isinstance(new_data, MutableSequence):\n                    new_data.append(new_elem)\n                elif isinstance(new_data, MutableSet):\n                    new_data.add(new_elem)\n                else:\n                    raise TypeError('Unknown container type encountered when removing private values from output')\n\n    return new_value\n\n\ndef _sanitize_keys_conditions(value, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals):\n    \"\"\" Helper method to sanitize_keys() to build deferred_removals and avoid deep recursion. \"\"\"\n    if isinstance(value, (text_type, binary_type)):\n        return value\n\n    if isinstance(value, Sequence):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableSequence):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = []  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        return new_value\n\n    if isinstance(value, Set):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableSet):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = set()  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        return new_value\n\n    if isinstance(value, Mapping):\n        if isinstance(value, MutableMapping):\n            new_value = type(value)()\n        else:\n            new_value = {}  # Need a mutable value\n        deferred_removals.append((value, new_value))\n        return new_value\n\n    if isinstance(value, tuple(chain(integer_types, (float, bool, NoneType)))):\n        return value\n\n    if isinstance(value, (datetime.datetime, datetime.date)):\n        return value\n\n    raise TypeError('Value of unknown type: %s, %s' % (type(value), value))\n\n\ndef sanitize_keys(obj, no_log_strings, ignore_keys=frozenset()):\n    \"\"\" Sanitize the keys in a container object by removing no_log values from key names.\n\n    This is a companion function to the `remove_values()` function. Similar to that function,\n    we make use of deferred_removals to avoid hitting maximum recursion depth in cases of\n    large data structures.\n\n    :param obj: The container object to sanitize. Non-container objects are returned unmodified.\n    :param no_log_strings: A set of string values we do not want logged.\n    :param ignore_keys: A set of string values of keys to not sanitize.\n\n    :returns: An object with sanitized keys.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    deferred_removals = deque()\n\n    no_log_strings = [to_native(s, errors='surrogate_or_strict') for s in no_log_strings]\n    new_value = _sanitize_keys_conditions(obj, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals)\n\n    while deferred_removals:\n        old_data, new_data = deferred_removals.popleft()\n\n        if isinstance(new_data, Mapping):\n            for old_key, old_elem in old_data.items():\n                if old_key in ignore_keys or old_key.startswith('_ansible'):\n                    new_data[old_key] = _sanitize_keys_conditions(old_elem, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals)\n                else:\n                    # Sanitize the old key. We take advantage of the sanitizing code in\n                    # _remove_values_conditions() rather than recreating it here.\n                    new_key = _remove_values_conditions(old_key, no_log_strings, None)\n                    new_data[new_key] = _sanitize_keys_conditions(old_elem, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals)\n        else:\n            for elem in old_data:\n                new_elem = _sanitize_keys_conditions(elem, no_log_strings, ignore_keys, deferred_removals)\n                if isinstance(new_data, MutableSequence):\n                    new_data.append(new_elem)\n                elif isinstance(new_data, MutableSet):\n                    new_data.add(new_elem)\n                else:\n                    raise TypeError('Unknown container type encountered when removing private values from keys')\n\n    return new_value\n\n\ndef heuristic_log_sanitize(data, no_log_values=None):\n    ''' Remove strings that look like passwords from log messages '''\n    # Currently filters:\n    # user:pass@foo/whatever and http://username:pass@wherever/foo\n    # This code has false positives and consumes parts of logs that are\n    # not passwds\n\n    # begin: start of a passwd containing string\n    # end: end of a passwd containing string\n    # sep: char between user and passwd\n    # prev_begin: where in the overall string to start a search for\n    #   a passwd\n    # sep_search_end: where in the string to end a search for the sep\n    data = to_native(data)\n\n    output = []\n    begin = len(data)\n    prev_begin = begin\n    sep = 1\n    while sep:\n        # Find the potential end of a passwd\n        try:\n            end = data.rindex('@', 0, begin)\n        except ValueError:\n            # No passwd in the rest of the data\n            output.insert(0, data[0:begin])\n            break\n\n        # Search for the beginning of a passwd\n        sep = None\n        sep_search_end = end\n        while not sep:\n            # URL-style username+password\n            try:\n                begin = data.rindex('://', 0, sep_search_end)\n            except ValueError:\n                # No url style in the data, check for ssh style in the\n                # rest of the string\n                begin = 0\n            # Search for separator\n            try:\n                sep = data.index(':', begin + 3, end)\n            except ValueError:\n                # No separator; choices:\n                if begin == 0:\n                    # Searched the whole string so there's no password\n                    # here.  Return the remaining data\n                    output.insert(0, data[0:begin])\n                    break\n                # Search for a different beginning of the password field.\n                sep_search_end = begin\n                continue\n        if sep:\n            # Password was found; remove it.\n            output.insert(0, data[end:prev_begin])\n            output.insert(0, '********')\n            output.insert(0, data[begin:sep + 1])\n            prev_begin = begin\n\n    output = ''.join(output)\n    if no_log_values:\n        output = remove_values(output, no_log_values)\n    return output\n\n\ndef _load_params():\n    ''' read the modules parameters and store them globally.\n\n    This function may be needed for certain very dynamic custom modules which\n    want to process the parameters that are being handed the module.  Since\n    this is so closely tied to the implementation of modules we cannot\n    guarantee API stability for it (it may change between versions) however we\n    will try not to break it gratuitously.  It is certainly more future-proof\n    to call this function and consume its outputs than to implement the logic\n    inside it as a copy in your own code.\n    '''\n    global _ANSIBLE_ARGS\n    if _ANSIBLE_ARGS is not None:\n        buffer = _ANSIBLE_ARGS\n    else:\n        # debug overrides to read args from file or cmdline\n\n        # Avoid tracebacks when locale is non-utf8\n        # We control the args and we pass them as utf8\n        if len(sys.argv) > 1:\n            if os.path.isfile(sys.argv[1]):\n                fd = open(sys.argv[1], 'rb')\n                buffer = fd.read()\n                fd.close()\n            else:\n                buffer = sys.argv[1]\n                if PY3:\n                    buffer = buffer.encode('utf-8', errors='surrogateescape')\n        # default case, read from stdin\n        else:\n            if PY2:\n                buffer = sys.stdin.read()\n            else:\n                buffer = sys.stdin.buffer.read()\n        _ANSIBLE_ARGS = buffer\n\n    try:\n        params = json.loads(buffer.decode('utf-8'))\n    except ValueError:\n        # This helper used too early for fail_json to work.\n        print('\\n{\"msg\": \"Error: Module unable to decode valid JSON on stdin.  Unable to figure out what parameters were passed\", \"failed\": true}')\n        sys.exit(1)\n\n    if PY2:\n        params = json_dict_unicode_to_bytes(params)\n\n    try:\n        return params['ANSIBLE_MODULE_ARGS']\n    except KeyError:\n        # This helper does not have access to fail_json so we have to print\n        # json output on our own.\n        print('\\n{\"msg\": \"Error: Module unable to locate ANSIBLE_MODULE_ARGS in json data from stdin.  Unable to figure out what parameters were passed\", '\n              '\"failed\": true}')\n        sys.exit(1)\n\n\ndef missing_required_lib(library, reason=None, url=None):\n    hostname = platform.node()\n    msg = \"Failed to import the required Python library (%s) on %s's Python %s.\" % (library, hostname, sys.executable)\n    if reason:\n        msg += \" This is required %s.\" % reason\n    if url:\n        msg += \" See %s for more info.\" % url\n\n    msg += (\" Please read the module documentation and install it in the appropriate location.\"\n            \" If the required library is installed, but Ansible is using the wrong Python interpreter,\"\n            \" please consult the documentation on ansible_python_interpreter\")\n    return msg\n\n\nclass AnsibleFallbackNotFound(Exception):\n    pass\n\n\nclass AnsibleModule(object):\n    def __init__(self, argument_spec, bypass_checks=False, no_log=False,\n                 mutually_exclusive=None, required_together=None,\n                 required_one_of=None, add_file_common_args=False,\n                 supports_check_mode=False, required_if=None, required_by=None):\n\n        '''\n        Common code for quickly building an ansible module in Python\n        (although you can write modules with anything that can return JSON).\n\n        See :ref:`developing_modules_general` for a general introduction\n        and :ref:`developing_program_flow_modules` for more detailed explanation.\n        '''\n\n        self._name = os.path.basename(__file__)  # initialize name until we can parse from options\n        self.argument_spec = argument_spec\n        self.supports_check_mode = supports_check_mode\n        self.check_mode = False\n        self.bypass_checks = bypass_checks\n        self.no_log = no_log\n\n        self.mutually_exclusive = mutually_exclusive\n        self.required_together = required_together\n        self.required_one_of = required_one_of\n        self.required_if = required_if\n        self.required_by = required_by\n        self.cleanup_files = []\n        self._debug = False\n        self._diff = False\n        self._socket_path = None\n        self._shell = None\n        self._syslog_facility = 'LOG_USER'\n        self._verbosity = 0\n        # May be used to set modifications to the environment for any\n        # run_command invocation\n        self.run_command_environ_update = {}\n        self._clean = {}\n        self._string_conversion_action = ''\n\n        self.aliases = {}\n        self._legal_inputs = []\n        self._options_context = list()\n        self._tmpdir = None\n\n        if add_file_common_args:\n            for k, v in FILE_COMMON_ARGUMENTS.items():\n                if k not in self.argument_spec:\n                    self.argument_spec[k] = v\n\n        # Save parameter values that should never be logged\n        self.no_log_values = set()\n\n        self._load_params()\n        self._set_fallbacks()\n\n        # append to legal_inputs and then possibly check against them\n        try:\n            self.aliases = self._handle_aliases()\n        except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:\n            # Use exceptions here because it isn't safe to call fail_json until no_log is processed\n            print('\\n{\"failed\": true, \"msg\": \"Module alias error: %s\"}' % to_native(e))\n            sys.exit(1)\n\n        self._handle_no_log_values()\n\n        # check the locale as set by the current environment, and reset to\n        # a known valid (LANG=C) if it's an invalid/unavailable locale\n        self._check_locale()\n\n        self._set_internal_properties()\n        self._check_arguments()\n\n        # check exclusive early\n        if not bypass_checks:\n            self._check_mutually_exclusive(mutually_exclusive)\n\n        self._set_defaults(pre=True)\n\n        # This is for backwards compatibility only.\n        self._CHECK_ARGUMENT_TYPES_DISPATCHER = DEFAULT_TYPE_VALIDATORS\n\n        if not bypass_checks:\n            self._check_required_arguments()\n            self._check_argument_types()\n            self._check_argument_values()\n            self._check_required_together(required_together)\n            self._check_required_one_of(required_one_of)\n            self._check_required_if(required_if)\n            self._check_required_by(required_by)\n\n        self._set_defaults(pre=False)\n\n        # deal with options sub-spec\n        self._handle_options()\n\n        if not self.no_log:\n            self._log_invocation()\n\n        # finally, make sure we're in a sane working dir\n        self._set_cwd()\n\n    @property\n    def tmpdir(self):\n        # if _ansible_tmpdir was not set and we have a remote_tmp,\n        # the module needs to create it and clean it up once finished.\n        # otherwise we create our own module tmp dir from the system defaults\n        if self._tmpdir is None:\n            basedir = None\n\n            if self._remote_tmp is not None:\n                basedir = os.path.expanduser(os.path.expandvars(self._remote_tmp))\n\n            if basedir is not None and not os.path.exists(basedir):\n                try:\n                    os.makedirs(basedir, mode=0o700)\n                except (OSError, IOError) as e:\n                    self.warn(\"Unable to use %s as temporary directory, \"\n                              \"failing back to system: %s\" % (basedir, to_native(e)))\n                    basedir = None\n                else:\n                    self.warn(\"Module remote_tmp %s did not exist and was \"\n                              \"created with a mode of 0700, this may cause\"\n                              \" issues when running as another user. To \"\n                              \"avoid this, create the remote_tmp dir with \"\n                              \"the correct permissions manually\" % basedir)\n\n            basefile = \"ansible-moduletmp-%s-\" % time.time()\n            try:\n                tmpdir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix=basefile, dir=basedir)\n            except (OSError, IOError) as e:\n                self.fail_json(\n                    msg=\"Failed to create remote module tmp path at dir %s \"\n                        \"with prefix %s: %s\" % (basedir, basefile, to_native(e))\n                )\n            if not self._keep_remote_files:\n                atexit.register(shutil.rmtree, tmpdir)\n            self._tmpdir = tmpdir\n\n        return self._tmpdir\n\n    def warn(self, warning):\n        warn(warning)\n        self.log('[WARNING] %s' % warning)\n\n    def deprecate(self, msg, version=None, date=None, collection_name=None):\n        if version is not None and date is not None:\n            raise AssertionError(\"implementation error -- version and date must not both be set\")\n        deprecate(msg, version=version, date=date, collection_name=collection_name)\n        # For compatibility, we accept that neither version nor date is set,\n        # and treat that the same as if version would haven been set\n        if date is not None:\n            self.log('[DEPRECATION WARNING] %s %s' % (msg, date))\n        else:\n            self.log('[DEPRECATION WARNING] %s %s' % (msg, version))\n\n    def load_file_common_arguments(self, params, path=None):\n        '''\n        many modules deal with files, this encapsulates common\n        options that the file module accepts such that it is directly\n        available to all modules and they can share code.\n\n        Allows to overwrite the path/dest module argument by providing path.\n        '''\n\n        if path is None:\n            path = params.get('path', params.get('dest', None))\n        if path is None:\n            return {}\n        else:\n            path = os.path.expanduser(os.path.expandvars(path))\n\n        b_path = to_bytes(path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n        # if the path is a symlink, and we're following links, get\n        # the target of the link instead for testing\n        if params.get('follow', False) and os.path.islink(b_path):\n            b_path = os.path.realpath(b_path)\n            path = to_native(b_path)\n\n        mode = params.get('mode', None)\n        owner = params.get('owner', None)\n        group = params.get('group', None)\n\n        # selinux related options\n        seuser = params.get('seuser', None)\n        serole = params.get('serole', None)\n        setype = params.get('setype', None)\n        selevel = params.get('selevel', None)\n        secontext = [seuser, serole, setype]\n\n        if self.selinux_mls_enabled():\n            secontext.append(selevel)\n\n        default_secontext = self.selinux_default_context(path)\n        for i in range(len(default_secontext)):\n            if i is not None and secontext[i] == '_default':\n                secontext[i] = default_secontext[i]\n\n        attributes = params.get('attributes', None)\n        return dict(\n            path=path, mode=mode, owner=owner, group=group,\n            seuser=seuser, serole=serole, setype=setype,\n            selevel=selevel, secontext=secontext, attributes=attributes,\n        )\n\n    # Detect whether using selinux that is MLS-aware.\n    # While this means you can set the level/range with\n    # selinux.lsetfilecon(), it may or may not mean that you\n    # will get the selevel as part of the context returned\n    # by selinux.lgetfilecon().\n\n    def selinux_mls_enabled(self):\n        if not HAVE_SELINUX:\n            return False\n        if selinux.is_selinux_mls_enabled() == 1:\n            return True\n        else:\n            return False\n\n    def selinux_enabled(self):\n        if not HAVE_SELINUX:\n            seenabled = self.get_bin_path('selinuxenabled')\n            if seenabled is not None:\n                (rc, out, err) = self.run_command(seenabled)\n                if rc == 0:\n                    self.fail_json(msg=\"Aborting, target uses selinux but python bindings (libselinux-python) aren't installed!\")\n            return False\n        if selinux.is_selinux_enabled() == 1:\n            return True\n        else:\n            return False\n\n    # Determine whether we need a placeholder for selevel/mls\n    def selinux_initial_context(self):\n        context = [None, None, None]\n        if self.selinux_mls_enabled():\n            context.append(None)\n        return context\n\n    # If selinux fails to find a default, return an array of None\n    def selinux_default_context(self, path, mode=0):\n        context = self.selinux_initial_context()\n        if not HAVE_SELINUX or not self.selinux_enabled():\n            return context\n        try:\n            ret = selinux.matchpathcon(to_native(path, errors='surrogate_or_strict'), mode)\n        except OSError:\n            return context\n        if ret[0] == -1:\n            return context\n        # Limit split to 4 because the selevel, the last in the list,\n        # may contain ':' characters\n        context = ret[1].split(':', 3)\n        return context\n\n    def selinux_context(self, path):\n        context = self.selinux_initial_context()\n        if not HAVE_SELINUX or not self.selinux_enabled():\n            return context\n        try:\n            ret = selinux.lgetfilecon_raw(to_native(path, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n        except OSError as e:\n            if e.errno == errno.ENOENT:\n                self.fail_json(path=path, msg='path %s does not exist' % path)\n            else:\n                self.fail_json(path=path, msg='failed to retrieve selinux context')\n        if ret[0] == -1:\n            return context\n        # Limit split to 4 because the selevel, the last in the list,\n        # may contain ':' characters\n        context = ret[1].split(':', 3)\n        return context\n\n    def user_and_group(self, path, expand=True):\n        b_path = to_bytes(path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n        if expand:\n            b_path = os.path.expanduser(os.path.expandvars(b_path))\n        st = os.lstat(b_path)\n        uid = st.st_uid\n        gid = st.st_gid\n        return (uid, gid)\n\n    def find_mount_point(self, path):\n
